title: Frontotemporal Dementia
authors: William W. Seeley, Bruce L. Miller

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD)
items.content: FTD manifests with three core clinical syndromes: behavioral variant FTD, semantic variant primary progressive aphasia (PPA), and nonfluent/agrammatic variant PPA. Behavioral variant FTD is characterized by social and emotional dysfunction such as apathy and disinhibition, often accompanied by executive control deficits. Semantic variant PPA involves a gradual loss of word and object meaning, while the nonfluent/agrammatic variant leads to difficulties in word production with motor speech impairment.
keyPoints: FTD is linked to frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD) pathology.
Commonly begins in the fifth to seventh decades of life.
Three core clinical syndromes: behavioral variant FTD, semantic variant PPA, nonfluent/agrammatic variant PPA.
Behavioral variant FTD symptoms include apathy, disinhibition, compulsivity, loss of empathy, and overeating.
Semantic variant PPA involves loss of word, object, person-specific, and emotion meaning.
Nonfluent/agrammatic variant PPA includes profound inability to produce words with motor speech impairment.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · Frontotemporal Dementia | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Anatomic Localization in Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD)
items.content: The clinical manifestations of FTD are determined by the specific brain regions affected. In behavioral variant FTD, degeneration occurs in the medial and orbital frontal cortex and anterior insula. Semantic variant PPA involves atrophy in the anterior temporal region, while nonfluent/agrammatic variant PPA affects the opercular frontal and precentral gyrus of the dominant hemisphere.
keyPoints: Behavioral variant FTD: degeneration occurs in medial/orbital frontal cortex and anterior insula.
Semantic variant PPA: atrophy in the anterior temporal region.
Nonfluent/agrammatic variant PPA: affects opercular frontal and precentral gyrus of dominant hemisphere.
Parietal functions like visuospatial processing and arithmetic calculations are typically unaffected even late in FTD syndromes.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · Frontotemporal Dementia | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Common Genetic Mutations and Their Pathogenic Mechanisms in Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD)
items.content: Mutations in C9orf72, GRN, and MAPT genes are significant in autosomal dominant FTD. The most common genetic cause is a hexanucleotide expansion in C9orf72, leading to various pathogenic features such as haploinsufficiency and TDP-43 inclusions. GRN mutations result in reduced progranulin levels due to mRNA degradation, implicating lysosomal dysfunction and neuroinflammation. MAPT mutations alter tau splicing or function, affecting microtubule binding.
keyPoints: C9orf72 hexanucleotide expansion (GGGGCC) is the most common genetic cause of familial/sporadic FTD, often presenting as bvFTD with/without MND and ALS.
GRN mutations lead to ~50% reduction in circulating progranulin protein levels due to mRNA degradation by nonsense-mediated decay.
MAPT mutations affect tau splicing or function, altering microtubule binding.
Progranulin is involved in tissue repair and tumor growth via TNF and sortilin receptors.

items.title: Clinical Implications of Genetic Mutations in FTD and Associated Syndromes
items.content: Mutations in C9orf72, GRN, and MAPT genes are associated with distinct clinical features. C9orf72 mutations often present with bvFTD and ALS, while MAPT and GRN mutations may include parkinsonian features but rarely ALS. Other rare genetic causes of FTD include VCP, TBK-1, TIA1, CHMP2b, TARDBP, and FUS gene mutations.
keyPoints: C9orf72 mutations often present with bvFTD and/or MND/ALS.
MAPT and GRN mutations may be associated with parkinsonian features; ALS is rare.
Rare genetic causes of autosomal dominant familial FTD include VCP, TBK-1, TIA1, CHMP2b, TARDBP, and FUS gene mutations.
FUS and TARDBP mutations primarily cause familial ALS but can be associated with FTD.

items.title: Pathological Hallmarks and Imaging of FTLD
items.content: FTLD is characterized by focal atrophy in the frontal, insular, and temporal cortex, visible through neuroimaging. This atrophy often begins focally before spreading to interconnected regions.
keyPoints: Focal atrophy of frontal, insular, and/or temporal cortex
Atrophy starts focally in one hemisphere, then spreads
~90% of FTLD cases have tau or TDP-43 protein inclusions

items.title: Protein Inclusions and Subtyping in FTLD
items.content: FTLD is subtyped based on the protein composition of neuronal and glial inclusions, primarily tau or TDP-43 (~90%), with a minority showing FET family proteins.
keyPoints: Microscopic findings: gliosis, microvacuolation, neuronal loss
~90% cases have tau or TDP-43 inclusions
Remaining ~10% show FET family protein inclusions

items.title: Pathogenesis and Misfolded Protein Propagation in FTLD
items.content: Misfolded tau, TDP-43, and FET proteins exhibit prion-like properties, facilitating disease spread through protein templating and propagation.
keyPoints: Misfolded tau's toxicity and spreading are critical for pathogenesis
TDP-43 and FET proteins have RNA/DNA binding roles
Prion-like properties enable exponential amplification of misfolding

items.title: Management of PSP-RS: Symptomatic Care and Caution with Antipsychotics
items.content: Treatment for Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP) is primarily symptomatic, focusing on managing associated symptoms such as depression and compulsions using SSRIs. Due to the common occurrence of parkinsonism in PSP, antipsychotics should be used cautiously as they can exacerbate motor symptoms.
keyPoints: Treatment for PSP-RS is symptomatic with no therapies known to slow progression or improve symptoms.
SSRIs may help manage depression and compulsions associated with FTD.
Antipsychotics must be used with caution in PSP due to potential exacerbation of parkinsonism.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.title: Distinguishing PSP-RS from Idiopathic Parkinson's Disease
items.content: PSP-RS can be confused with idiopathic Parkinson's disease (PD), but key differences include the presence of downgaze paresis and other abnormalities in voluntary eye movements typical of PSP, which are not seen in PD. Dementia is more likely to occur in PD patients as they age or if there are severe extrapyramidal signs.
keyPoints: PSP-RS involves falls, executive dysfunction, and a progressive oculomotor syndrome with square wave jerks and slowed saccades.
Distinguishing features from PD include downgaze paresis and abnormalities in voluntary eye movements.
Dementia is more likely in PD patients with increasing age, severity of extrapyramidal signs, long disease duration, or presence of depression.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.title: Prognosis and Pathology of PSP-RS
items.content: PSP-RS is a progressive condition with death occurring within 5–10 years from onset. The pathology involves accumulation of hyperphosphorylated 4-repeat tau in neurons and glia, with prominent involvement in specific brain regions such as the subthalamic nucleus and substantia nigra.
keyPoints: Death occurs within 5–10 years of PSP-RS onset.
Pathology includes hyperphosphorylated 4-repeat tau accumulation in neurons and glia.
Prominent involvement in subthalamic nucleus, globus pallidus, substantia nigra, among others.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Differential Diagnosis of Corticobasal Syndrome (CBS)
items.content: CBS is a progressive disorder characterized by asymmetric rigidity, dystonia, myoclonus, apraxia, and alien limb phenomena. It can be due to various underlying pathologies including CBD, PSP, FTLD-TDP, and AD.
keyPoints: CBS presents with asymmetric rigidity, dystonia, myoclonus, apraxia, and alien limb phenomena.
Eventually becomes bilateral leading to dysarthria, slow gait, action tremor, and frontal-predominant dementia.
Differential diagnosis includes CBD, PSP, FTLD-TDP, and AD (up to 30% of CBS cases).
Important to distinguish clinical syndrome from specific histopathologic entities.

items.title: Histopathology and Treatment of Corticobasal Syndrome (CBS)
items.content: In CBD, CBS's underlying pathology includes ballooned, achromatic, tau-positive neurons; astrocytic plaques; and dystrophic glial tau pathomorphologies. There are no disease-modifying therapies for CBS; treatment remains symptomatic.
keyPoints: Histopathology of CBD in CBS: ballooned, achromatic, tau-positive neurons; astrocytic plaques; axonal threads; oligodendroglial coiled bodies.
Overlap with PSP pathology noted.
No disease-modifying therapies available for CBS; treatment is symptomatic.

items.question: Which protein aggregations are primarily implicated in frontotemporal dementia (FTD)?
items.explanation: FTD is associated with abnormal protein aggregation of tau or transactive response DNA-binding protein of 43 kDa (TDP-43).
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · Frontotemporal Dementia | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: In the behavioral variant of frontotemporal dementia, which symptom is NOT typically observed?
items.explanation: The behavioral variant of FTD includes symptoms like apathy, disinhibition, loss of empathy, but not hyperactivity.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · Frontotemporal Dementia | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which clinical syndrome is characterized by a profound inability to produce words and often has prominent motor speech impairment?
items.explanation: The nonfluent/agrammatic variant of primary progressive aphasia is characterized by a profound inability to produce words and often has prominent motor speech impairment.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · Frontotemporal Dementia | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which gene mutation is the most common genetic cause of familial or sporadic frontotemporal dementia (FTD) and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)?
items.explanation: The hexanucleotide expansion in C9orf72 is the most common genetic cause of familial or sporadic FTD and ALS.

items.question: Mutations in which gene lead to a reduction in circulating progranulin protein levels due to nonsense-mediated decay?
items.explanation: Mutations in the GRN gene lead to a reduction in circulating progranulin protein levels due to nonsense-mediated decay.

items.question: Which of the following genes is associated with mutations that cause changes in tau molecule splicing or function?
items.explanation: Mutations in the MAPT gene lead to changes in the alternate splicing of tau or cause loss of function, affecting microtubule binding.

items.question: Which neuroimaging finding is often profound at autopsy in patients with Frontotemporal Lobar Degeneration (FTLD)?
options: Focal atrophy of frontal, insular, and/or temporal cortex
items.explanation: The passage states that focal atrophy of the frontal, insular, and/or temporal cortex is a hallmark of FTLD.

items.question: In Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD), which neurotransmitter system remains relatively spared compared to Alzheimer's Disease?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the cholinergic system is relatively spared in FTD, unlike Alzheimer's Disease.

items.question: What percentage of patients with FTLD show underlying pathology composed primarily of tau or TDP-43 proteins?
items.explanation: The passage notes that ~90% of patients have inclusions containing either tau or TDP-43.

items.question: What is the primary role of TDP-43 and FET family proteins in neurons as described in the passage?
options: Chaperoning mRNAs to dendritic spines for translation
items.explanation: The passage describes TDP-43 and FET family proteins as chaperoning mRNAs to the distal neuron for translation within dendritic spines.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is NOT typically associated with Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP)?
items.explanation: The response to L-dopa is limited or absent in PSP.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.question: Which of the following features distinguishes PSP from idiopathic Parkinson's disease (PD) regarding eye movements?
options: Development of downgaze paresis in PSP
Absence of square wave jerks in PSP
Presence of oculocephalic reflexes in both conditions
items.explanation: PSP is characterized by the development of downgaze paresis, unlike PD.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.question: What is a common feature in PSP that contributes to frequent falls?
items.explanation: Frequent unexplained falls are due to a combination of axial rigidity, inability to look down, and impaired judgment.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.question: Which type of dementia is often overlapped with PSP?
items.explanation: The dementia in PSP overlaps with bvFTD, featuring apathy and frontal-executive dysfunction.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.question: Which of the following is a common initial presentation in patients with corticobasal syndrome (CBS)?
options: Asymmetric rigidity, dystonia, myoclonus, and apraxia
Acute onset of unilateral limb paralysis
items.explanation: CBS typically presents with asymmetric rigidity, dystonia, myoclonus, and apraxia.

items.question: Which pathological feature is most specific to corticobasal degeneration (CBD) in CBS?
options: Lewy bodies in the substantia nigra
items.explanation: CBD features ballooned, achromatic, tau-positive neurons.

items.question: Which condition is NOT typically associated with corticobasal syndrome (CBS)?
items.explanation: Huntington's disease is not typically associated with CBS.

items.statement: Visuospatial processing deficits are typically observed late in the course of FTD syndromes.
items.explanation: Parital functions such as visuospatial processing are typically unaffected even late in the FTD syndromes.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · Frontotemporal Dementia | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Autosomal dominant inheritance is seen in 10–20% of all FTD cases.
items.explanation: Autosomal dominant inheritance occurs in only 10-20% of FTD cases.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · Frontotemporal Dementia | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Homozygous GRN mutations result in neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis.
items.explanation: Homozygous GRN mutations lead to neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis, highlighting the lysosome's role in molecular dysfunction.

items.statement: Mutations in MAPT and GRN genes are commonly associated with ALS.
items.explanation: MAPT and GRN mutations are often associated with parkinsonian features, whereas ALS is rare.

items.statement: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors are effective in treating Frontotemporal Dementia due to the involvement of the cholinergic system.
items.explanation: The passage states that acetylcholinesterase inhibitors have poor efficacy in FTD because the cholinergic system is relatively spared.

items.statement: Molecular PET imaging with ligands specific to misfolded tau protein shows robust and specific binding in FTLD-tau.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that these ligands only show robust and specific binding to AD-related misfolded tau, not FTLD-tau.

items.statement: PSP-RS is characterized by a prominent response to L-dopa treatment.
items.explanation: The response to L-dopa in PSP is limited or absent.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.statement: Tau-positive glial inclusions are an essential feature of PSP.
items.explanation: Prominent tau-positive glial inclusions, like tufted astrocytes, are a signature lesion in PSP.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.statement: CBS can be due to Alzheimer's disease (AD) in up to 30% of cases.
items.explanation: AD accounts for up to 30% of CBS in some series.

items.statement: Treatment for CBS includes disease-modifying therapies.
items.explanation: Treatment of CBS remains symptomatic; no disease-modifying therapies are available.

items.assertion: Patients with the nonfluent/agrammatic variant of PPA develop a profound inability to produce words.
items.reason: This is due to degeneration in the opercular frontal and precentral gyrus of the dominant hemisphere.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · Frontotemporal Dementia | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: FTD can be reliably diagnosed based on clinical features alone.
items.reason: Clinical features do not allow reliable prediction of the underlying FTLD subtype or major molecular class for all syndromes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the reason states that clinical features are insufficient for predicting FTLD subtypes.

items.assertion: Misfolded proteins in neurodegenerative diseases have prion-like properties.
items.reason: They can template the misfolding of their natively folded counterparts, promoting exponential amplification and spread.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the prion-like nature of misfolded proteins.

items.assertion: PSP is often confused with idiopathic Parkinson's disease.
items.reason: Both conditions can present with restricted upgaze in elderly patients.
items.explanation: Although both may have restricted upgaze, PSP develops downgaze paresis and other eye movement abnormalities not seen in PD.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.assertion: Dementia is a common feature of Progressive Supranuclear Palsy.
items.reason: The dementia overlaps with bvFTD, featuring apathy and frontal-executive dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why dementia is common in PSP.
items.reference: Frontotemporal Dementia · TREATMENT | PROGRESSIVE SUPRANUCLEAR PALSY SYNDROME

items.assertion: CBS is pathognomonic for corticobasal degeneration (CBD).
items.reason: CBS can be due to CBD, PSP, FTLD-TDP, and AD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because CBS is not exclusively pathognomonic for CBD.
